transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
I think you're on mute.
Can you hear me?
Hey, Marcel.
Sorry.
How are you, man?
Really good.
You caught me on a crazy day because it's investor day.
We got so much inbound after our Series A that I was just like, I can't respond to all these people.
So I just had my EA book everybody on the same day and just be like, I'll have one day.
I can set aside one day.
That's it.
And you can talk for a while.
Oh, my God.
Virtual and in person.
And so you're my 12th meeting of the day.
But I was like, no, no.
Texted me.
I dropped the ball.
I'm responding to him.
I told Dick, like, please book something ASAP.
I'm so good.
Yeah.
Dude, you're the epitome of the founder.
Look at you.
You're cliche.
But you're  killing it, which is great.
So it's exciting.
It's really, really hard, but really rewarding, and I wouldn't have it any other way, man.
Amazing.
But it's hard.
I can imagine you're living there.
How have been?
How was that?
You were in Hawaii, I think you said, right?
It was, yeah, yeah, yeah.
It was funny.
I did a month over there, two weeks working, and then two weeks playing, and that was good.
Then I got back, thinking I'd be here in the U.S.
for a while.
Then the next week, I had to go to Bogota for a week, because we just finished the acquisition of PayU, which essentially doubles the size of the company.
So I was in meetings all week down there, inherited a bunch of people, all that fun.
Then finally just got back, and then I had to go personal, had to go visit my dad.
He doesn't live here.
He lives in Nevada, and I sat down other day, and I was like, wow, it's just weird to sit in one place and not go somewhere.
Ah, man.
I can't complain.
Everything's good.
Yeah, that's insane.
That sounds like a lot.
Traveling for me these days, like we went on a camping trip this weekend and it was just like we left Friday afternoon, just an Airstream down to Santa Cruz, you know, have our Airstream.
But like even those things, I'm just like, it's like a lot of work.
That sounds exhausting, your wife.
I know, vacations give me anxiety.
Like the thought of taking vacation right now gives me more anxiety than actually.
Oh my gosh.
Wow.
Well, you know, it certainly looks good from the outside.
So hopefully, you know, the growth keeps going and, you know, you guys are killing it.
Yeah, it's been fun.
Like if we have a few minutes today, I can show you some of the stuff that we're building that very, very, very, very soon we'll launch to customers to be able to have access to it as well.
And so it's like, but we'd love to like know like what's up with you, like what can we do?
Yeah, by the way, I didn't realize this, but there was a little bit of a scheduling mix up.
No big deal.
But I just didn't that.
So tomorrow.
Oh, Marc and I are joining you on the listening tour.
Oh, perfect.
Okay, cool.
And we can do that.
If you want to save any of that, you don't need to.
I can even give you back some time.
The thing I want to chat with you about is a very small, quick thing.
just wanted to see if you're interested in helping me on something.
But if, you know, if you need time back, it's an exhausting day.
not at all.
This is, like, honestly, like, this is what I actually enjoy is, like, talking to customers, friends, and, like, people.
And, like, VC is, like, the thing I have to do because we need more money.
And they control billions of dollars, and you need to play the game.
Like, that's just, you know.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It's, like, if you can stop to hammer me on the terms and just sort of, like, come along, like, you'll still do really well.
So, knock it off with the VCs.
But, anyway, so the reason I really wanted to chat with you is, so I've got, so the way marketing is organized on my team, it's, obviously, it's a global team.
I've got about 30 people in marketing, and I've got, essentially, seven leaders that do different things, demand gen, and, you know.
Yeah.
Columns and brands stuff like
What Mark does, he does webbing content.
So I'm getting the team together, I'm doing something different this year.
Usually I get my team together towards the end of the year and we plan for the next year.
And that's usually in some remote location, which is the case again this year because we are all over the world.
Bottom line is I'm bringing my team together earlier in the year.
I'm finding that it's actually better to do it earlier.
We're going to meet in Vienna, Austria.
It turned out to be the easiest place to do it.
always like to get somewhere fun.
But one of the things I'm doing is it's a three-day offsite.
We're just talking about our business and what we're doing and our plans and blah, blah, blah.
We do do a lot at Rapyd as a company in AI.
The thing we're doing with you is really cool and obviously we'll spend time on that.
But we're doing big things in AI on the operation of the business.
It's significant what we're doing with AI in terms of how we're running Rapids' core business of payments.
Yep, yep.
All good stuff.
So I am...
Trying to move my team along and get them comfortable.
Marc is like the star.
He is the...
He's great.
He's great.
And he's really into it, which is amazing.
I'm somewhat into it, trying to get myself more into it.
I understand the reasoning why.
I've got a lot of really talented people that kind of use it, but it's sort of not that amazing.
And I'm really trying to move them forward.
So I've got a couple of sessions at the offsite planned more on focus on AI.
I would like to see if you could dial in for 30 minutes.
Yeah, man.
That would be so cool.
It would be amazing.
And really, I'll tell you the reason why, Marcel, I've thought about lots of people.
But the fact that you're a serial ex-CMO, I think, but now you're the founder of an AI company.
And obviously, you're helping us, which is almost secondary.
I think they would really respect the fact that like, here's a founder of a company doing huge things.
Yeah, you're helping us, but you're actually one of us.
Yeah.
Exactly.

And also, like, we get a lot of insight, like, from, for instance, like, working with this company, like, Sunbit, you know, and then there's this other company, we're doing Augment Code, which is kind of like a cursor competitor, but then we're working with Webflow and Ramp.
And, like, so we're seeing, like, all these companies in all these stages.
There's these e-commerce companies, like, we're talking to the RealReal, we're talking to SentinelOne, and then Abnormal Security as a customer.
And, you know, it's like Outreach just signed up for a customer.
Or, Okta just signed up for a customer.
Like, all these customers.
And then you can kind of see all their insights.
Yeah, yeah.

Not that they're IP, but just, like, you see how they operate, and you see the, where they're doing well, where they're not, where their people are good, where, like, the teams are a mess, and, you know, where they have their  together.
And, you know, it just gives us a lot of perspective, too, you know.
Which is amazing.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
So, I don't know if you'd be free.
We could change things around if needed, but the way we were thinking, ideally, it would be the...
We're there the 23rd to the 25th of July.
And this session we were going to try and do at the end of the day in Vienna, which would be your morning in California here, our morning.
Which day is the 23rd or which day?
The 24th actually.
So it would be the second day and it would be from 515 to 545 in the evening there.
Sorry, 515 to 545 there.
So it would be 815 in the morning here.
I'm usually in the office by 630.
Okay.
So you're already up and around.
an early person.
today I had by 8 a.m.
I already had had four meetings.
But I catch all the New York people in the morning.
Let me block my calendar.
So it would be 815.
So I'll do 8 a.m.
to 9 a.m.
my time.
That would be awesome.
Yeah, maybe there's a little flex in there.
there.
I'll send you a separate invite, too.
Yeah, that would be awesome.
what I was thinking, too, Marcel, was I haven't fully thought it through, and maybe we can even chat with Mark for a minute tomorrow on it, too.
But he always has great ideas, too.
But I would love yours, too.
I would love to make it kind of a little bit about your story up front, just a few minutes.

I can't even send you an email on this, and we can think it through a touch, but a little bit on your story about how you kind of got to hear, like, Path as a CMO, and then getting into the CEO, and then what it was about AI that you saw.
And then once we kind of maybe do that part, maybe we could do a little bit on, you know, where you see, less about how you're helping us now, because they all understand that.
More about, like, more about you could see...
And...

AI fitting in, in marketing in like quantum ways, because I've got people that work in product marketing and demand gen and brand and partner marketing, you know, all, all those different disciplines, marketing operations, reporting, and it's like, as a serial CMO, that's now the founder, like, what's like that, man, like, this is where you're like, people should really start to pay attention if you, if you have that kind of.
Yeah, yeah, and like, I did a similar session for a friend of mine at merge.dev, and for her team, marketing team, and I think like, I can even send you the recording of it if you want to.
I love it.
Like, you know, just don't share publicly, like, don't post it on LinkedIn.
Just between you and us.
Just between you and us.

Not even if you wanted to share, but it's like, I think what really resonated, it wasn't about tools, it wasn't about us, it wasn't about selling growthx, it was just like, like, two things that I think are the most most foundational things for anybody, including.
Marketers.
One is first principles thinking and understanding how to distill things down to the foundation and understanding the essence of how things work.
So it's like, oh, yeah, I just sent this email.
But like, no, no, no, hold on.
Now, why did you send it this way?
How did you think about it?
How did you connect the dots?

Like, every work product has all this messy middle that if you figure out, like, how it all connects, and then you go down to the essence of the ingredients, if you will, and you can break it down to, like, that essence, and then rebuild it back out from a first principles, that's what's necessary in order to, like, really think about how applying AI is, like, decomposing everything down to the molecules, you know, to the atoms, right?
And then the second thing is delegation.
And then the third one is writing, but I'll go into that.
then delegation is, like, if you're really  at sharing a context, and you don't know how to delegate something to a fellow person, like, you're going to be really crappy using AI forever, right?
Like, and so...
What does it take for you to get someone with no context to perform as good as you on something?
You need to enable them.
You need to give them the right context.
need to show them where to go fetch the right inputs.
You need to help them figure out how to come up with the right plans, how to execute against those plans.
You need to figure out how to evaluate if something is good or not, how to think about it critically, where to think about it critically, right?
Where to get feedback, who can come along, and all those things.
And that's delegation 101.

So if you're a delegation, you don't know how to delegate, which most people have never had to delegate in their lives, then you're really bad at giving instructions to an LLM or to AI or anything, right?
And the last thing is like a lot of marketers.
How many times in our careers as CMOs heard our team members say, I'm just not very good at writing.
Like how many people in my career, 90% of people I've ever hired all said that, right?
I'm not really good at numbers.
Oh, I'm not really technical.
And it's just like, if anyone says that word, it's just like, I'm sorry, like all the information is out there.
You have to be really good at writing.
You actually have to be really technical, not because you need to code, but because you need to understand things from first principles.
You can't be afraid.
These things are not, like, crazy hard to understand, you know?
And so, anyway, sorry, I got excited, but, like, those things are just so, like, principles-driven.
They're not about tools.
They're not about access to information.
They're not about, like, it applies to every person, you know?
Yeah, yeah.
Well, I think it would be interesting to, like, have you kind of share, like, that framework of how you think about it.
And then also maybe, you know, like, I think one of the challenges I see with some of my team, some people like Marc, like, they just, they embrace it.
They get into it.
They're, you know, naturally curious.
They're doing their things.
I've got other people on my team that are really good at what they do, but they just don't have time to go mess with it.
They're, honestly, they're not technical.
They're not going to initially be CMOs, but they're their thing, you know?
And so it's sort of, like, how can I give them?

A way to think about how do you take something as vast and consequential as AI and help them start to bring it down to places you can use it to start to have whatever, 10x returns in your time or have outsized returns of things that you're working on because you're using it versus not taking the time to figure it out.
I feel like there's something there.
And so, yeah, I'm just really trying to kind of give these guys, you know.
Yeah, and I think there's also like, I mean, this is a little bit kind of like early, right?
Because we're migrating to our own platform right now on a lot of these things, right?
But like, you know, and we'll soon give this access to like a lot of your teams or whatever, you know.
But I think like what's kind of interesting here is like if you look at this, right, like this is a like assignment brief and you go.
Into, like, our workflow generation, you know, this is, like, an entire workflow that's, like, an AI coding agent built, right?
And this is not technical and whatever, but it does, like, a lot of things, right?
Like, it's fetching a bunch of, like, it's doing a lot, right?
But, like, when you break it down to the essence, like, a lot of what makes this good or bad, it comes down to, like, I like to call them artifacts now, right?
And so, like, let me show you, because we're still migrating, but let me show you one that I was just, like, working on.
But, like, engine.com, they're a customer, right?
Like, here's an artifact on, like, writing guidelines for them that we created from, like, understanding.

And so, in a lot of ways, like, if I took this artifact as is, and I literally just did this, and I copy and paste it into anything you want, like, literally anything at all, like, it would help you do that better, right?
So, if you can have, like, team-wide artifact, and pretty soon, like, we'll give you our whole team access to this, so then, like, they can use this as well.

But even if you're just using Chat2PT, Cloud, or whatever y'all want to use, then we can all can generate like these artifacts that are shared artifacts that are just like really, really good context.
Then at that point, it's like, okay, it's like take Engine, like the artifact.
I'll show you a notion because it's a little bit better.
But like you look at this, and it's just like really well written, good context, right?
It has like a lot of like that distilled down that's like just really good.
And so then like using that then enables people to use a lot of the tools out there, but in a way that's way more context aware, essentially, you know?

So like these little things are like, it is a little bit tooling, but it's also just how you approach it a little bit, which is like spending a little bit more time on the plant, spend a lot more time on the artifacts, and then your outputs will just be like 100 times better, you know?
Yeah, we've been a little bit of this, but I think like you've like next level, and I think we're just sort of like kind of messing around.
And I haven't really kind of institutionalized it where here you're like building a whole product around it and whatever.
But I think this direction is like the right way.
I think one of the big challenges is just sort of like, how does one think about like the idea of like creating artifacts, you know, and like, you know, why should you do it?
And how would you do it?
And how would it help you?
And here's why you need to do it and stuff like that.
So I think it's even like those things are good, but that's really cool, by the way.
I can't wait to get our hands on it so we can start to really kind of crank it up.
I know, like we're so close, but this is super helpful.
I have to run, but okay, so tomorrow we'll talk some more and we'll try to reserve some time.
I have a ton of ideas.
I also have a lot of things I've shared in the past.
Okay.
If you want to just send me a calendar invite and then.
Yeah, and send me that podcast, by the way, or the recording you made.
Yeah, yeah.
And we'll, yeah, we'll, and we'll sync up and we can talk a little bit tomorrow after the listening.
Perfect.
Perfect.
Thank you, Mark.
Ben, glad we could connect.
By the way, how was your, how was the launch party?
I'm sorry I had to miss that.
It was, it was really good.
Are you, are you in town on the 25th?
Oh, when?
The next Wednesday.
I'm around, yeah.
Yeah?
Okay, so let me send you, I'll send it to you on Slack, but we're actually doing a CMO dinner, if you're around.
I just wasn't sure, because like, yeah, I know you're traveling, you were traveling quite a bit, but it's going to be a Lamar.
Oh, yeah, Lamar's great.
Oh, here we go.
Yeah, so we got a ton of awesome CMOs, so like, it would be awesome if you want to join.
I would love it, man.
Yeah, like right now we have 32 people, RSVP, so we're expecting like 30 to 40, probably.
You know how it is, like there's always some drop off at the last minute, but so far it's like really good CMOs, you know, of like really awesome companies.
Perfect.
I would love to, let me double check everything, but it sounds good.
I'll double check and I can confirm on there.
Yeah, yeah, that'll be perfect.
And then I'll find the video and send it to you on Slack, if that's okay.
Cool, that'd be fantastic.
I would appreciate it.
Whenever, take your time.
But get to chat with you, and we'll sync tomorrow.
See you, Marc.
All right, talk to you then.
All right, see ya.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss Marc Winitz's request for Marcel Santilli to speak at Rapyd's marketing team offsite, and explore AI applications in marketing.](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Marcel agreed to speak at Rapyd's marketing team offsite in Vienna on July 24th, 8:15-8:45 AM PST](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=454.0)
  - [Marcel emphasized three key principles for AI adoption: first principles thinking, delegation skills, and strong writing abilities](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=601.0)
  - [GrowthX is developing AI-powered tools to create shared "artifacts" that provide context and improve team-wide outputs](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=818.0)
  - [A CMO dinner event is planned for July 25th, with 30-40 attendees expected](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=1030.0)

## Topics

### Rapyd's Marketing Team Offsite

  - [Scheduled for July 23-25 in Vienna, Austria](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=249.0)
  - [30-person global marketing team with 7 leaders in various disciplines](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=231.0)
  - [Marc aims to increase team's comfort and proficiency with AI](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
  - [Requested Marcel to speak for 30 minutes on AI in marketing](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=329.0)

### Marcel's Proposed Speaking Topics

  - [Personal journey from CMO to AI company founder](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=342.0)
  - [How AI fits into various marketing disciplines (product marketing, demand gen, brand, partner marketing, etc.)](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=540.0)
  - [Quantum leap opportunities for AI in marketing](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=745.0)

### Key Principles for AI Adoption in Marketing

1.  First principles thinking
      - [Distilling tasks to their essence](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=611.0)
      - [Understanding foundational elements of work products](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=616.0)
2.  Delegation skills
      - [Providing context and enabling others to perform tasks](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
      - [Critical for effective AI tool usage](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=687.0)
3.  Strong writing abilities
      - [Essential for clear communication and instruction to AI tools](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=720.0)

### GrowthX AI-Powered Tools

  - [Developing workflow generation and AI coding agents](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=840.0)
  - [Creating shared "artifacts" for improved context and team-wide outputs](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=854.0)
  - [Example shown: writing guidelines for Engine.com](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=919.0)
  - [Emphasis on spending more time on planning and artifact creation for better outputs](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=937.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Marcel to send calendar invite for the July 24th speaking engagement](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=474.0)
  - [Marcel to share recording of a similar AI session he conducted for merge.dev](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=1083.0)
  - [Marc to confirm attendance for the CMO dinner on July 25th](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=1074.0)
  - [Marcel to send CMO dinner details via Slack](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=1030.0)
  - [Further discussion planned for tomorrow's meeting to refine speaking topics and approach](https://fathom.video/share/QQ3kYwdFQ1zr3fZx8_qgz_WB8CRFX82T?tab=summary&timestamp=994.0)

action_items.description: Send calendar invite to Marcel for July 24th Vienna offsite speaking slot (8:15-8:45am PT)
Send Marc recording of merge.dev marketing team AI session via Slack
Send Marc CMO dinner details for June 25th event at Lamar via Slack
Check schedule and confirm attendance for June 25th CMO dinner
